src/app/page.tsx: This is the main dashboard — the first screen users see after logging in. It pulls together three key pieces: a grid of project cards showing each project's name and progress, an activity feed showing what the team has been doing recently, and a stats bar with totals like open tasks and overdue items. The dashboard uses the useAuth hook to check the user is logged in and useProjects to fetch their project list from Supabase.
src/components/TaskBoard.tsx: The TaskBoard is the heart of the project view — a Kanban board with columns for each status (To Do, In Progress, Review, Done). Users drag task cards between columns to update their status, which immediately saves to the database via the tasks API. It uses a custom drag-and-drop hook for smooth interactions and subscribes to real-time updates so if a teammate moves a card, you see it instantly.
src/components/Dashboard.tsx: The Dashboard component organises the home screen layout. It renders three sections in a responsive grid: ProjectCard components for each project the user belongs to, the ActivityFeed showing recent team actions, and a StatsBar with summary numbers. It acts as a layout orchestrator — it doesn't fetch data itself but receives it from the page via hooks.
src/components/ActivityFeed.tsx: The ActivityFeed shows a chronological list of recent team actions: tasks created, status changes, comments, and file uploads. It connects to Supabase's real-time channels via the useRealtime hook, so new activities appear instantly without refreshing. Each entry shows who did what, when, with relative timestamps like '2 minutes ago'.
src/hooks/useAuth.tsx: The useAuth hook manages everything about the current user's session. It provides the logged-in user object, a signIn function that calls the auth API, a signOut function that clears the session, and automatic token refresh. It wraps the Supabase auth client and exposes a clean interface that any component can use to check if the user is authenticated.
src/hooks/useRealtime.ts: This hook creates a live connection to Supabase's real-time engine. When a database table changes (new row, update, delete), this hook fires a callback so the UI updates instantly. It handles subscribing when the component mounts and cleaning up the subscription when it unmounts. Used by useProjects, useTasks, and ActivityFeed.
src/app/api/tasks/route.ts: The tasks API handles all task operations. GET returns tasks for a project (with optional status/assignee filters), POST creates a new task and optionally asks the AI assistant to suggest subtasks, PATCH updates a task's status, priority, or assignee. Every request checks permissions first — only project members can read or write tasks.
src/app/api/auth/route.ts: The authentication API has two endpoints. POST handles login: it takes an email and password, validates them against Supabase Auth, and returns a signed JWT token. GET returns the current session — the frontend calls this on page load to check if the user's token is still valid. Invalid or expired tokens get a 401 response.
src/services/ai-assistant.ts: The AI assistant service wraps OpenAI's API to provide smart features throughout the app. It has three main functions: summarizeProject takes all tasks in a project and generates a progress report, suggestTasks analyses the project context and recommends next actions, and a general query function for natural-language search across tasks and comments.
src/services/stripe.ts: The Stripe client handles all payment processing. It exports a configured Stripe SDK instance, a createCheckoutSession function for starting new subscriptions, and a handleWebhook function that verifies and processes incoming Stripe events (payment succeeded, subscription cancelled, etc). All sensitive operations happen server-side only.
src/lib/supabase.ts: This file creates two Supabase clients: a regular one for frontend use (respects row-level security) and an admin one for server-side operations that need to bypass security rules. Both connect to the same Supabase project but with different permission levels. Every database query in the app flows through one of these clients.
src/lib/permissions.ts: The permissions module checks whether a user can perform a specific action. checkPermission takes a user ID, a resource (project or task), and an action (view, edit, delete) and returns true/false. requireAdmin is a stricter check that throws an error if the user isn't a workspace admin. Used by all API routes before modifying data.
